one_line_takeaway: Cross-user prefix and semantic caching in LLM serving is a practical timing side channel: an unprivileged API user can measure prefill-time drops to confirm cache hits and reconstruct victims' exact inputs (62% disease recovery, up to 100% field-hit detection) or infer RAG query topics (43-100% across legal domains), so cache isolation, rate limiting, or timing obfuscation are needed.
key_finding: Cache-sharing performance optimizations leak private user inputs through measurable prefill-time differences, and the leakage is severe enough to reconstruct EXACT inputs under prefix caching. The Time Analyzer reaches 87.13% accuracy in predicting cache-hit block counts (1600-token prompts, 600 samples, Gradient Boosting) and up to 100% field-level hit-detection accuracy; for semantic caching it distinguishes hit vs. miss with 100% accuracy. Under real-world constraints, the medical attack extracts exact disease inputs with up to 62% success (Probability-based Vocabulary) and best overall exact-field recovery of 12.50% (GaussianNB). For legal RAG, semantic-content extraction success ranges 43%-100% across 13 legal domains. A notable secondary finding: fine-tuned LLM constructors look strong in the Ideal setting (54% disease ASR) but collapse to 0% under constraints due to output variability and excessive attempts/memory -- traditional ML methods (GaussianNB) are more constraint-robust than LLM-based construction for this precise-matching task.
core_contribution: InputSnatch is a timing-based side-channel attack that steals other users' private prompts from LLM inference services by exploiting cache-sharing optimizations. The paper's OWN contribution (distinct from cited token-length/embedding-inversion work) is threefold: (1) the first systematic investigation of timing side channels created by cross-user cache sharing in LLM serving, covering two mechanisms -- prefix caching (reuses KV-cache attention states for identical prompt prefixes) and semantic caching (reuses responses for embedding-similar queries); (2) an attack framework with two co-designed components -- an Input Constructor that narrows the intractable input search space using field-correlation learning from open-source datasets, and a Time Analyzer that maps observed prefill/TTFT time to the number of cache-hit blocks via statistical fitting with outlier removal; and (3) an end-to-end demonstration under realistic API constraints (rate limits, memory caps, cache TTL) that recovers exact user inputs (prefix caching) or infers query topics/semantics (semantic caching). The key insight is that prefix caching enables EXACT input reconstruction because of strict block-level prefix matching, letting the attacker rebuild inputs field-by-field: once field i is confirmed hit via a timing observation, the search space for field i+1 collapses using learned correlations (e.g., age/gender -> likely disease -> likely symptoms).
threat_model: A realistic, unprivileged adversary who accesses the victim LLM service as an ordinary API/web user. The attacker has NO white-box access -- unaware of training data, model parameters, or hardware -- and can only send prompts and measure response times (specifically prefill time / Time-To-First-Token via streaming). The victim is another user whose sensitive prompt is cached on the same computing node under a shared cache. Because a user's requests are routed to the same node for cache reuse, the attacker gets repeated attempts. The attacker constructs candidate inputs to force a cache hit; a measured timing drop signals a match, leaking the victim's input content. The paper stresses the attack works even with encrypted channels because the leakage is at the system-optimization layer, not the transport layer, so TEEs/homomorphic encryption do not defend against it. Operating constraints modeled: OpenAI Tier-2 rate limit of 5,000 requests/min, ~250K-token KV-cache memory budget, and a 5-minute cache TTL bounding attack duration. The authors explicitly scope OUT user attribution: the attack recovers input content but cannot bind it to a specific identity.
limitations: Stated by the authors: (1) The attack lacks fine-grained information for fully precise reconstruction -- it captures secret content but not the exact detailed context, so exact complete-input recovery is low (12.5% overall under constraints); the authors suggest combining with embedding-inversion or token-length side channels. (2) No user attribution -- inferred inputs cannot be linked to a specific user or object, which the authors admit significantly limits practical exploitation. Reviewer-observed gaps: all experiments run on LOCAL vLLM/GPTCache deployments or the OpenAI org platform rather than adversarial attacks against live production multi-tenant APIs, so real-world node co-location, cross-tenant routing, and scheduling noise at scale are simulated rather than demonstrated end-to-end; symptom/overall exact-recovery rates are modest (single-digit to ~13%); the medical and legal datasets are Chinese-domain (ChatDoctor patient-doctor dialogues, CrimeKgAssitant), and the constructor's field-correlation assumptions (e.g., structured six-field form, fields separated by >=16 tokens) may not transfer to free-form prompts. The Fine-tuned LLM's total failure under constraints also shows the framework's constructor choice is brittle.

datasets_used: ChatDoctor -- 110K real patient-doctor conversations; distilled via ChatGPT-4o into a 16,276-sample Formatted Input Field dataset (6 fields), search space >2x10^42
CrimeKgAssitant -- 200,000 legal question-answer pairs across 13 legal domains (used to train the semantic-caching constructor)
Medical test set -- 200 samples held out from Formatted Input Field + 200 ChatGPT-4-generated samples
Legal test set -- 30 ChatGPT-4-generated queries + 30 held-out queries from the training corpus
quantitative_results: Time Analyzer: 87.13% cache-hit-block prediction accuracy (1600-token prompts, 600 samples, Gradient Boosting); 86.34% for 800-token prompts (600 samples, Gradient Boosting)
Time Analyzer field-level hit detection: up to 100% (1600 tokens, >=300 samples); 97.25% at 800 tokens
Semantic-cache Time Analyzer: 100% accuracy distinguishing cache hits vs misses
Medical prefix-cache attack, Ideal setting (Probability-based Vocabulary): 67.5% disease ASR, 53.75% symptom ASR, 49% overall ASR
Medical prefix-cache attack, constrained (All) setting: 62% disease ASR (Probability-based Vocabulary); best overall exact-field ASR 12.50% (GaussianNB) with 54% disease / 13.5% symptom
Fine-tuned LLaMA-3 8B constructor: 54% disease ASR Ideal but 0% under All constraints (excessive attempts/memory/time)
GaussianNB constrained cost: 1086 +/- 898 attempts, 40,207 tokens, 276s; ~2x more efficient than the random baseline
Legal RAG semantic-cache attack: ASR 43.33% (Infringement) up to 100% (Traffic Accidents, Property Disputes, Demolition/Resettlement) across 13 domains
Reported infrastructure numbers: shared long prefixes give 70-90% memory reduction; semantic caching handles 20-30% (up to ~31%) of queries in production
baselines_compared: Input Constructor variants (the paper's own internal comparison): Baseline random construction, Gaussian Naive Bayes, Probability-based Vocabulary (word-level), Fine-tuned LLaMA-3 8B
Time Analyzer ML methods: Gradient Boosting vs Random Forest vs XGBoost
Evaluation scenarios: Ideal (unconstrained) vs All (memory 250K tokens + rate limit 5,000 RPM + 5-min TTL)
Prior-art context cited (NOT re-implemented as baselines): Weiss et al. token-length side channel, Morris et al. logit/embedding inversion, Debenedetti et al. privacy side channels
